title: SEGMENT 9 — David Bennett: the first pig heart, January 2022

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — David Bennett: the first pig heart, January 2022
I want to tell you a story.
The story is set in a hospital room at the University of Maryland Medical Center, in Baltimore, in January 2022.
The patient is a 57-year-old man named David Bennett.
He's been in the hospital for six weeks.
He has end-stage heart failure.
He's on a machine called ECMO — extracorporeal membrane oxygenation — which is essentially a portable heart-lung bypass that keeps him alive while his own heart is no longer pumping effectively.
He cannot get up.
He cannot eat.
He is, in the most literal sense, sustained by tubes.
He is not eligible for a human heart transplant.
There are several reasons, but the principal one is a record of poor compliance with previous medical treatments — earlier in his life, he had not consistently taken his medications.
Transplant programs use compliance as a criterion because a transplanted human heart requires daily, lifelong immunosuppression, and a patient who won't take their pills will reject the graft.
So the human transplant list is closed to him.
Without a transplant, he will die in days, possibly hours.
The team at Maryland, led by Dr.
Bartley Griffith, the surgeon, and Dr.
Muhammad Mohiuddin, the immunologist who had spent decades on xenotransplantation, present David Bennett with an option.

Under an FDA pathway called expanded access — sometimes called compassionate use — the agency can authorize, on a one-patient basis, the use of an experimental therapy in a patient with no other options and a high likelihood of death.
Maryland had filed paperwork.
Revivicor had a 10-edit pig ready.
The FDA, after weeks of back-and-forth, had given a single-patient authorization.
Bennett, after long conversations with his family and the medical team, agreed.

On January 7, 2022, in an eight-hour operation, Bartley Griffith and his surgical team removed David Bennett's native heart and replaced it with the heart of a Revivicor pig — a 10-gene-edited animal whose three xenoantigens were knocked out and which carried six human transgenes.
The pig had been sacrificed earlier that morning, in an adjacent building.

Its heart had been kept on a perfusion device — a machine called the OCS Heart, made by a company called TransMedics, which pumps oxygenated blood through an organ outside the body to keep it viable during transport.
The pig heart was transported across the medical campus, brought into the operating room, examined, and sewn into David Bennett's chest.
The clamps came off.
The new heart, the pig heart, started beating.
It pinked up.
It contracted.
It did not turn black.
There was no hyperacute rejection.
The fifty-year-old wall, the alpha-gal cliff, had not been hit.
The team closed the chest.
Bennett was wheeled to the ICU.
By the next morning, he was awake, able to talk, able to recognize his family.
He could move his hands.
He had a pig's heart inside him and he was alive.
The world found out the next day.
The story exploded.
Front page of every newspaper.
Every news network.
It was, depending on your perspective, either the dawn of a new medical era or a Frankenstein nightmare.
The medical community was, in the main, cautiously excited.
Surgical xenotransplantation had moved from preclinical primate experiments into a human patient.
The proof was alive.
For the next several weeks, Bennett continued to improve.
He could sit up.
He spoke with the press.
He watched the Super Bowl.
He told the team he was grateful, that he was happy to be a pioneer.
He understood the risk.
He knew his survival was not guaranteed.
The team was carefully monitoring his immune function, his renal function, his cardiac output.
Everything looked good.
Then, around day 40, things began to deteriorate.
The pig heart started to show signs of damage — thickening of the muscle, signs of cellular distress.
Bennett's overall condition worsened.
The team escalated treatment.
On day 60, on March 8, 2022, David Bennett died.
The autopsy and detailed post-mortem analysis took months, and the findings were published the following year.
The story they tell is humbling.
The principal finding was not, in fact, classical immune rejection.
The pig heart's tissue did not show the hallmarks of T-cell attack or antibody-mediated rejection in the way the team had feared.

What the autopsy showed was something more subtle: evidence that the pig heart had been infected with porcine cytomegalovirus, or pCMV, a herpesvirus that lives in pigs, and that the infection had compromised the heart muscle from within.
This was a surprise, because the donor pig had been screened for pCMV before transplant.
The screening test that had been used was a blood-based PCR, which detects circulating virus — but pCMV, like other cytomegaloviruses, can sit latent in tissues without showing up in the blood.
The Revivicor herd's screening protocol, as it existed in early 2022, had a blind spot.
The team learned this after the fact.
The screening protocol has since been updated to use tissue-based detection.
This was the most important lesson of the Bennett case, and it's worth pausing on.
Bennett's heart was destroyed not by immune rejection but by a pig virus that the screening missed.
The immune problem — the problem the field had spent fifty years on — had been substantially solved by the genetic engineering.
The new problem was infectious.
The Bennett experience told the field: it's not enough to engineer the pig.
You also have to surveil it.
We'll come back to the gnotobiotic herd question in segment 15.
David Bennett's family, after his death, said publicly that they had no regrets.
The team at Maryland received both criticism and credit.
The trial was, by the metric the team set in advance — survival to discharge, evidence that the genetic engineering had cleared the hyperacute hurdle — a partial success.
The man lived sixty days with a pig's heart.
He lived two months he otherwise would not have had.
The field had its first proof.
Tragic, but proof.
The Maryland team performed a second pig heart transplant in September 2023, into a different patient named Lawrence Faucette.
He lived six weeks before succumbing to rejection.
The second case taught additional lessons about the immune protocol but did not produce the same paradigm shift as the first.
After Faucette, the heart program paused while the kidney program — which had been preparing in parallel — moved into clinical use.
That was 2024.
And 2024 was a remarkable year.
